figures.caption: Figure 5 Visualization of pseudo depth and scene-flow preparation on LIBERO (Liu et al., 2023) and RoboCasa (Nasiriany et al., 2024) demonstrations. The first two rows show LIBERO depth maps and flow fields, while the last two rows show RoboCasa depth maps and flow fields. These visualizations illustrate the pseudo geometric supervision used to construct 3D scene-flow targets for future Gaussian prediction.

Figure 1 Comparison of manipulation policy paradigms. Compared with 2D policies, 3D-enhanced policies, and video/latent world models, GaussianDream uses current Gaussian reconstruction and future Gaussian prediction to learn structured 3D supervision from robot videos while retaining efficient prefix-based action generation.

Figure 9 Additional qualitative visualization of current Gaussian reconstruction and future Gaussian prediction. Each example compares the observed frame and pseudo depth target with the reconstructed current geometry and predicted future Gaussian states. The visualization illustrates that GaussianDream captures both current scene layout and short-horizon geometric changes.
Table 5 Core notation for GaussianDream and its Gaussian reconstruction/prediction modules.
Table 6 Training notation used in the GaussianDream and action objectives.

Table 1 Success rates (%) on LIBERO (Liu et al., 2023). GaussianDream achieves the best Spatial and Goal performance and a 98.4% average success rate; Spatial Forcing is reported using its PyTorch implementation.
Table 2 Success rates (%) on RoboCasa Human-50 (Nasiriany et al., 2024). GaussianDream achieves the best average result and the strongest Pick&Place performance.

Figure 2 Overview of GaussianDream. denotes frozen modules, and denotes trainable modules. (a) Inputs include the language instruction, robot state, current observation, temporal context frames, and learnable GaussianDream queries. (b) The GaussianDream encoder extracts VGGT patch tokens from temporal observations and lets them interact with GaussianDream queries through the TGE module. (c) The GaussianDream decoder contains a static Gaussian head for current reconstruction and a dynamic Gaussian head for future prediction. (d) Current and future RGB/depth renderings provide dense supervision for reconstruction and prediction. (e) The GaussianDream action policy conditions on the learned prefix to generate robot actions. (f) The TGE module alternates token interaction and temporal aggregation through self-attention, temporal attention, and an MLP.

Figure 8 Real-robot inference visualization. Compared with the π0.5 (Physical Intelligence et al., 2025) baseline, GaussianDream produces smoother execution trajectories by conditioning action generation on the learned GaussianDream prefix.

Figure 6 Real-robot hardware setup. The evaluation platform uses a policy-controlled follower arm mounted on a mobile base, together with an agent-view camera and a wrist-mounted camera for visual observation. During teleoperation-based demonstration collection, a separate leader arm is manually operated by the user, and its motions are transferred to the follower arm. During autonomous evaluation, GaussianDream directly controls the follower arm from language instructions and visual observations.
